cue: How do you say you have a hunch?

es.meaning: I have a feeling that; something tells me.
es.exampleTranslation: I have a feeling it'll rain today.
es.regionNote: Distinctly Mexican — latir as 'to have a hunch / to appeal' is MX colloquial; Spain says 'me da que'. Also 'me late' alone = 'sounds good to me'. Reviewer to confirm register breadth.

it.meaning: I have a feeling that; I suspect.
it.exampleTranslation: I have a feeling it'll rain today.

bridge.note: Two bodies guessing: Italian's hunch KNOWS (mi sa, from sapere — impersonal knowledge arriving on its own), Mexico's hunch BEATS (me late, from latir — the heart as forecaster). Both are dative constructions where the feeling happens TO you, which is exactly the grammar English lacks. A hunch you receive, not one you make.

interference.es_to_it: 'Mi late che' is not Italian — the organ doesn't forecast there; knowledge does: mi sa che. And mi sa che takes indicative, not subjunctive.
interference.it_to_es: 'Me sabe que' means something tastes to me — the Spanish hunch beats: me late que (in Mexico; elsewhere 'me da que').

review.notes: From Joe's Italian Matters marker list 2026-07-20 (discovery source only; all examples freshly authored). ES pairing by Claude, UNVERIFIED. Dative-experiencer pair; MX-anchored.
